title: Retinal Vein Occlusion (CRVO / BRVO)
oneLiner: Sudden, painless vision loss from blockage of a retinal vein; associated with hypertension, diabetes, glaucoma – requires urgent workup for underlying vascular risk.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Hypertensive emergency (malignant hypertension):** RVO can be the presenting feature of severely elevated BP causing acute end-organ damage.  
  *Finding:* BP >180/120 mmHg + headache, papilloedema, chest pain, confusion, or acute renal impairment.  
  *Action:* Immediate referral to medical team for controlled BP lowering (IV labetalol or nicardipine per local protocol) – do not lower BP too fast (>25% in first 24h) to avoid further retinal ischaemia.
- **Giant cell arteritis (GCA):** Older patient (>50) with sudden vision loss; must be treated before temporal artery biopsy.  
  *Finding:* Headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, ESR >50 mm/h (often >100), CRP markedly raised.  
  *Action:* Start high-dose prednisolone 40–60 mg oral immediately – do not wait for biopsy. If missed, bilateral blindness, stroke, aortic dissection.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Sudden, painless, unilateral vision loss (scotoma or blurring) – onset over hours to days  
  - Description: “curtain” or “shadow” in part of visual field (BRVO) or entire field (CRVO)  
  - May report floaters if associated vitreous haemorrhage  
  - Pain is absent unless secondary neovascular glaucoma develops (weeks to months later)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Pupils: relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) if ischaemic CRVO; usually no RAPD in non-ischaemic or BRVO  
  - Fundoscopy:  
    - CRVO: “blood and thunder” appearance – diffuse retinal haemorrhages in all four quadrants, dilated and tortuous retinal veins, optic disc oedema, cotton-wool spots  
    - BRVO: sectoral haemorrhages, often superotemporal, respecting horizontal midline  
  - Visual acuity: variable – may be 6/6 (non-ischaemic BRVO) to hand movements (ischaemic CRVO)  
  - Anterior segment: clear initially; later may show rubeosis iridis (neovascularisation) in ischaemic CRVO  
  - Intraocular pressure (IOP): normal at presentation; elevated in neovascular glaucoma
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO):** sudden profound vision loss, cherry red spot, RAPD, retinal whitening (no haemorrhages) – differentiates by absence of haemorrhages and presence of retinal opacity  
- **Non-ischaemic CRVO vs ischaemic CRVO:**  
  - Ischaemic CRVO has RAPD, extensive capillary non-perfusion on fluorescein angiogram, poor visual acuity (<6/60) – worse prognosis  
- **Diabetic retinopathy:** typically bilateral, dot-blot haemorrhages, microaneurysms, no sectoral distribution, and history of diabetes  
- **Ocular ischaemic syndrome (carotid disease):** low IOP, dilated retinal veins, mid-peripheral dot haemorrhages, chronic symptoms – check carotid bruit, imaging (carotid duplex)  
- **Vitreous haemorrhage (from other cause):** obscured fundus, ultrasound needed – no retinal haemorrhage pattern  
- **Splitting feature:** The single best differentiator between RVO and CRAO:  
  - **Fundoscopy:** haemorrhages (RVO) vs. retinal whitening + cherry red spot (CRAO)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - Blood pressure (seated and standing)  
  - Visual acuity (Snellen chart)  
  - Pupillary examination (assess for RAPD)  
  - IOP measurement (tonometry) – rule out neovascular glaucoma  
  - Confrontation visual fields (document scotoma)  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, ESR, CRP – to screen for GCA, polycythaemia, thrombophilia  
  - U&Es, creatinine – assess renal function, especially if hypertensive  
  - Glucose, HbA1c – exclude diabetes  
  - Cholesterol profile – for cardiovascular risk  
  - Thrombophilia screen (e.g., lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, protein C/S, factor V Leiden) – only if young (<50), bilateral, or no vascular risk factors  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: **Fluorescein angiography (FFA)** – defines ischaemic vs non-ischaemic, detects neovascularisation, guides laser treatment  
  - Second-line: **Optical coherence tomography (OCT)** – quantifies macular oedema (thickened retina, cystoid spaces) – monitors anti-VEGF response  
  - **Carotid Doppler** – if suspect ocular ischaemic syndrome (carotid stenosis)  
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood pressure measurement** – undiagnosed hypertensive crisis is the most common co-morbidity and can be acutely fatal. Also, **ESR/CRP** in any patient >50 with vision loss – do not start steroids for GCA without labs but treat empirically if high suspicion.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ward):**  
  - Rule out GCA (start prednisolone if suspected) and hypertensive emergency (lower BP per protocol)  
  - Urgent ophthalmology referral (same-day)  
  - No direct intervention for the occlusion itself – no antiplatelet or anticoagulation therapy proven beneficial and may worsen haemorrhage  
  - Manage elevated IOP (if neovascular glaucoma) with topical drops (e.g., timolol 0.5% bd) – but real therapy is anti-VEGF or laser  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Complete an ophthalmology review including FFA/OCT  
  - **If ischaemic CRVO with neovascularisation:** panretinal photocoagulation (PRP) laser  
  - **If macular oedema (CRVO or BRVO):** intravitreal anti-VEGF injection (ranibizumab [Lucentis] or aflibercept [Eylea]) – typically monthly initially  
  - Control vascular risk: optimise BP (<140/90), HbA1c (<7% [53 mmol/mol]), statin (atorvastatin 20–80 mg daily depending on CV risk)  
  - Consider antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 75 mg daily) only if concurrent cardiovascular disease – no evidence for RVO recurrence prevention, but often used for primary prevention in this high-risk group. **Check with ophthalmology.**  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Follow-up in ophthalmology macular clinic for serial OCT and continued anti-VEGF if needed (often monthly loading then tapered)  
  - Monitor for neovascular glaucoma (IOP checks, gonioscopy) – treat with PRP + anti-VEGF  
  - Lifestyle counselling: smoking cessation, dietary modification, exercise  
  - Consider thrombophilia referral if young or bilateral  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: nil specific unless hypertensive management needed (avoid aggressive IV fluids)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: routine low-molecular-weight heparin if not contraindicated (bleeding risk is low from RVO itself)  
  - Diet: normal diet with low salt (if hypertensive)  
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly; monitor BP daily  
  - Activity: no restrictions
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Anti-VEGF intravitreal (first-line for macular oedema):**  
  - Ranibizumab (Lucentis): 0.5 mg intravitreal injection monthly for 3–6 months, then as needed  
  - Aflibercept (Eylea): 2 mg intravitreal injection monthly for 3 months, then 2-monthly  
  - *Dose route:* Intravitreal injection only, performed by ophthalmologist. Do not prescribe on ward.  
- **Topical steroids (for cystoid macular oedema if anti-VEGF not available):**  
  - Dexamethasone intravitreal implant (Ozurdex) – 0.7 mg implant, lasts up to 6 months  
  - *Use:* for persistent macular oedema despite anti-VEGF  
- **Systemic steroids for GCA:**  
  - Prednisolone 40–60 mg oral once daily until biopsy and onward weaning  
- **Antihypertensives (initiate only after clarifying BP with medical team):**  
  - Amlodipine 5–10 mg oral once daily, or ramipril 2.5–10 mg oral once daily – start low, titrate  
  - For hypertensive emergency: IV labetalol – [check local policy]  
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**  
  - Anti-VEGF: avoid in active ocular infection, recent intraocular surgery within 2 weeks  
  - Systemic steroids in GCA: avoid live vaccines; monitor blood glucose, bone density  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Ranibizumab and aflibercept – no dose adjustment (intravitreal, negligible systemic absorption)  
  - Ramipril – reduce dose if eGFR <30 mL/min, monitor K+  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Intravitreal anti-VEGF overdose** (multi-dose vials): never inject from a vial meant for multiple uses. Use single-use prefilled syringes only.  
  - **Prednisolone in GCA:** delaying or omitting loading dose can lead to blindness. Always give first dose immediately.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - **Macular oedema** – main cause of visual loss in non-ischaemic CRVO and BRVO; treat with anti-VEGF  
  - **Neovascular glaucoma** – within weeks to months (ischaemic CRVO); presents with pain, red eye, elevated IOP, rubeosis iridis; requires urgent PRP  
  - **Vitreous haemorrhage** – from neovascularisation; may obscure view, needs B-scan ultrasound  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Persistently reduced vision from macular damage  
  - **Neovascularisation of retina or iris** – risk of recurrent vitreous bleed and tractional retinal detachment  
  - **Carotid artery disease progression** – especially if ocular ischaemic syndrome suspected  
  - Increased cardiovascular mortality (RVO is a marker of systemic vascular disease) – manage risk factors
sections.Prognosis:
- **BRVO:** 50–70% recover vision spontaneously if non-ischaemic; ischaemic BRVO has poorer outcome but responds well to anti-VEGF.  
- **CRVO:** non-ischaemic: 50% maintain 6/12 or better; ischaemic: <20% achieve 6/60 without treatment. Anti-VEGF improves outcomes in both.  
- **Overall:** Mortality is not increased directly from RVO but from associated cardiovascular disease – 5-year mortality ~10% higher than age-matched controls.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always measure IOP** in any new RVO – neovascular glaucoma can develop silently and is reversible if caught early  
- **Examine both eyes** – bilateral RVO suggests systemic vasculitis, hyperviscosity, or severe hypertension  
- **Check for carotid bruits** – unilateral RVO + bruit = urgent carotid duplex (ocular ischaemic syndrome may require revascularisation)
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT start aspirin or warfarin** “to thin the blood” – no benefit and increases risk of vitreous haemorrhage. Antiplatelet/anticoagulation should only be for concurrent systemic indications (e.g., AF) after ophthalmology clearance.  
- **Do NOT use mannitol or acetazolamide** unless IOP is >30 mmHg or neovascular glaucoma confirmed – these will not help the occlusion and can cause electrolyte disturbances  
- **Do NOT delay GCA treatment** while waiting for biopsy – if clinical suspicion is high, start steroids immediately.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening vision (sudden decrease, new scotoma)  
  - Development of eye pain, redness, or headache  
  - Flashes or floaters (new vitreous haemorrhage)  
  - Any systemic symptoms: headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication (GCA)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Ophthalmology clinic within 1–2 weeks for formal assessment (FFA/OCT)  
  - GP within 1–2 weeks for BP, glucose, cholesterol management if not admitted  
  - Consider cardiovascular risk assessment (QRISK3 score) and statin if not already on one
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call ophthalmology reg (immediately) for:**  
  - Suspected ischaemic CRVO with RAPD and VA <6/60  
  - Any new eye pain / red eye (neovascular glaucoma)  
  - Vitreous haemorrhage obscuring view  
  - Raised IOP >25 mmHg  
- **Call medical or stroke team for:**  
  - BP >180/110 mmHg with symptoms (hypertensive emergency)  
  - Suspected GCA: start steroids and contact rheumatology/ophthalmology  
  - New neurological symptoms (stroke or TIA) – consider urgent brain imaging

rawMarkdown:
## Retinal Vein Occlusion (CRVO / BRVO)
**One-liner:** Sudden, painless vision loss from blockage of a retinal vein; associated with hypertension, diabetes, glaucoma – requires urgent workup for underlying vascular risk.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Hypertensive emergency (malignant hypertension):** RVO can be the presenting feature of severely elevated BP causing acute end-organ damage.  
  *Finding:* BP >180/120 mmHg + headache, papilloedema, chest pain, confusion, or acute renal impairment.  
  *Action:* Immediate referral to medical team for controlled BP lowering (IV labetalol or nicardipine per local protocol) – do not lower BP too fast (>25% in first 24h) to avoid further retinal ischaemia.
- **Giant cell arteritis (GCA):** Older patient (>50) with sudden vision loss; must be treated before temporal artery biopsy.  
  *Finding:* Headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, ESR >50 mm/h (often >100), CRP markedly raised.  
  *Action:* Start high-dose prednisolone 40–60 mg oral immediately – do not wait for biopsy. If missed, bilateral blindness, stroke, aortic dissection.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Sudden, painless, unilateral vision loss (scotoma or blurring) – onset over hours to days  
  - Description: “curtain” or “shadow” in part of visual field (BRVO) or entire field (CRVO)  
  - May report floaters if associated vitreous haemorrhage  
  - Pain is absent unless secondary neovascular glaucoma develops (weeks to months later)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Pupils: relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) if ischaemic CRVO; usually no RAPD in non-ischaemic or BRVO  
  - Fundoscopy:  
    - CRVO: “blood and thunder” appearance – diffuse retinal haemorrhages in all four quadrants, dilated and tortuous retinal veins, optic disc oedema, cotton-wool spots  
    - BRVO: sectoral haemorrhages, often superotemporal, respecting horizontal midline  
  - Visual acuity: variable – may be 6/6 (non-ischaemic BRVO) to hand movements (ischaemic CRVO)  
  - Anterior segment: clear initially; later may show rubeosis iridis (neovascularisation) in ischaemic CRVO  
  - Intraocular pressure (IOP): normal at presentation; elevated in neovascular glaucoma

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO):** sudden profound vision loss, cherry red spot, RAPD, retinal whitening (no haemorrhages) – differentiates by absence of haemorrhages and presence of retinal opacity  
- **Non-ischaemic CRVO vs ischaemic CRVO:**  
  - Ischaemic CRVO has RAPD, extensive capillary non-perfusion on fluorescein angiogram, poor visual acuity (<6/60) – worse prognosis  
- **Diabetic retinopathy:** typically bilateral, dot-blot haemorrhages, microaneurysms, no sectoral distribution, and history of diabetes  
- **Ocular ischaemic syndrome (carotid disease):** low IOP, dilated retinal veins, mid-peripheral dot haemorrhages, chronic symptoms – check carotid bruit, imaging (carotid duplex)  
- **Vitreous haemorrhage (from other cause):** obscured fundus, ultrasound needed – no retinal haemorrhage pattern  
- **Splitting feature:** The single best differentiator between RVO and CRAO:  
  - **Fundoscopy:** haemorrhages (RVO) vs. retinal whitening + cherry red spot (CRAO)

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - Blood pressure (seated and standing)  
  - Visual acuity (Snellen chart)  
  - Pupillary examination (assess for RAPD)  
  - IOP measurement (tonometry) – rule out neovascular glaucoma  
  - Confrontation visual fields (document scotoma)  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, ESR, CRP – to screen for GCA, polycythaemia, thrombophilia  
  - U&Es, creatinine – assess renal function, especially if hypertensive  
  - Glucose, HbA1c – exclude diabetes  
  - Cholesterol profile – for cardiovascular risk  
  - Thrombophilia screen (e.g., lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, protein C/S, factor V Leiden) – only if young (<50), bilateral, or no vascular risk factors  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: **Fluorescein angiography (FFA)** – defines ischaemic vs non-ischaemic, detects neovascularisation, guides laser treatment  
  - Second-line: **Optical coherence tomography (OCT)** – quantifies macular oedema (thickened retina, cystoid spaces) – monitors anti-VEGF response  
  - **Carotid Doppler** – if suspect ocular ischaemic syndrome (carotid stenosis)  
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood pressure measurement** – undiagnosed hypertensive crisis is the most common co-morbidity and can be acutely fatal. Also, **ESR/CRP** in any patient >50 with vision loss – do not start steroids for GCA without labs but treat empirically if high suspicion.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ward):**  
  - Rule out GCA (start prednisolone if suspected) and hypertensive emergency (lower BP per protocol)  
  - Urgent ophthalmology referral (same-day)  
  - No direct intervention for the occlusion itself – no antiplatelet or anticoagulation therapy proven beneficial and may worsen haemorrhage  
  - Manage elevated IOP (if neovascular glaucoma) with topical drops (e.g., timolol 0.5% bd) – but real therapy is anti-VEGF or laser  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Complete an ophthalmology review including FFA/OCT  
  - **If ischaemic CRVO with neovascularisation:** panretinal photocoagulation (PRP) laser  
  - **If macular oedema (CRVO or BRVO):** intravitreal anti-VEGF injection (ranibizumab [Lucentis] or aflibercept [Eylea]) – typically monthly initially  
  - Control vascular risk: optimise BP (<140/90), HbA1c (<7% [53 mmol/mol]), statin (atorvastatin 20–80 mg daily depending on CV risk)  
  - Consider antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 75 mg daily) only if concurrent cardiovascular disease – no evidence for RVO recurrence prevention, but often used for primary prevention in this high-risk group. **Check with ophthalmology.**  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Follow-up in ophthalmology macular clinic for serial OCT and continued anti-VEGF if needed (often monthly loading then tapered)  
  - Monitor for neovascular glaucoma (IOP checks, gonioscopy) – treat with PRP + anti-VEGF  
  - Lifestyle counselling: smoking cessation, dietary modification, exercise  
  - Consider thrombophilia referral if young or bilateral  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: nil specific unless hypertensive management needed (avoid aggressive IV fluids)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: routine low-molecular-weight heparin if not contraindicated (bleeding risk is low from RVO itself)  
  - Diet: normal diet with low salt (if hypertensive)  
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly; monitor BP daily  
  - Activity: no restrictions

### Drugs & Doses
- **Anti-VEGF intravitreal (first-line for macular oedema):**  
  - Ranibizumab (Lucentis): 0.5 mg intravitreal injection monthly for 3–6 months, then as needed  
  - Aflibercept (Eylea): 2 mg intravitreal injection monthly for 3 months, then 2-monthly  
  - *Dose route:* Intravitreal injection only, performed by ophthalmologist. Do not prescribe on ward.  
- **Topical steroids (for cystoid macular oedema if anti-VEGF not available):**  
  - Dexamethasone intravitreal implant (Ozurdex) – 0.7 mg implant, lasts up to 6 months  
  - *Use:* for persistent macular oedema despite anti-VEGF  
- **Systemic steroids for GCA:**  
  - Prednisolone 40–60 mg oral once daily until biopsy and onward weaning  
- **Antihypertensives (initiate only after clarifying BP with medical team):**  
  - Amlodipine 5–10 mg oral once daily, or ramipril 2.5–10 mg oral once daily – start low, titrate  
  - For hypertensive emergency: IV labetalol – [check local policy]  
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**  
  - Anti-VEGF: avoid in active ocular infection, recent intraocular surgery within 2 weeks  
  - Systemic steroids in GCA: avoid live vaccines; monitor blood glucose, bone density  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Ranibizumab and aflibercept – no dose adjustment (intravitreal, negligible systemic absorption)  
  - Ramipril – reduce dose if eGFR <30 mL/min, monitor K+  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Intravitreal anti-VEGF overdose** (multi-dose vials): never inject from a vial meant for multiple uses. Use single-use prefilled syringes only.  
  - **Prednisolone in GCA:** delaying or omitting loading dose can lead to blindness. Always give first dose immediately.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - **Macular oedema** – main cause of visual loss in non-ischaemic CRVO and BRVO; treat with anti-VEGF  
  - **Neovascular glaucoma** – within weeks to months (ischaemic CRVO); presents with pain, red eye, elevated IOP, rubeosis iridis; requires urgent PRP  
  - **Vitreous haemorrhage** – from neovascularisation; may obscure view, needs B-scan ultrasound  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Persistently reduced vision from macular damage  
  - **Neovascularisation of retina or iris** – risk of recurrent vitreous bleed and tractional retinal detachment  
  - **Carotid artery disease progression** – especially if ocular ischaemic syndrome suspected  
  - Increased cardiovascular mortality (RVO is a marker of systemic vascular disease) – manage risk factors

### Prognosis
- **BRVO:** 50–70% recover vision spontaneously if non-ischaemic; ischaemic BRVO has poorer outcome but responds well to anti-VEGF.  
- **CRVO:** non-ischaemic: 50% maintain 6/12 or better; ischaemic: <20% achieve 6/60 without treatment. Anti-VEGF improves outcomes in both.  
- **Overall:** Mortality is not increased directly from RVO but from associated cardiovascular disease – 5-year mortality ~10% higher than age-matched controls.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always measure IOP** in any new RVO – neovascular glaucoma can develop silently and is reversible if caught early  
- **Examine both eyes** – bilateral RVO suggests systemic vasculitis, hyperviscosity, or severe hypertension  
- **Check for carotid bruits** – unilateral RVO + bruit = urgent carotid duplex (ocular ischaemic syndrome may require revascularisation)

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT start aspirin or warfarin** “to thin the blood” – no benefit and increases risk of vitreous haemorrhage. Antiplatelet/anticoagulation should only be for concurrent systemic indications (e.g., AF) after ophthalmology clearance.  
- **Do NOT use mannitol or acetazolamide** unless IOP is >30 mmHg or neovascular glaucoma confirmed – these will not help the occlusion and can cause electrolyte disturbances  
- **Do NOT delay GCA treatment** while waiting for biopsy – if clinical suspicion is high, start steroids immediately.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening vision (sudden decrease, new scotoma)  
  - Development of eye pain, redness, or headache  
  - Flashes or floaters (new vitreous haemorrhage)  
  - Any systemic symptoms: headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication (GCA)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Ophthalmology clinic within 1–2 weeks for formal assessment (FFA/OCT)  
  - GP within 1–2 weeks for BP, glucose, cholesterol management if not admitted  
  - Consider cardiovascular risk assessment (QRISK3 score) and statin if not already on one

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call ophthalmology reg (immediately) for:**  
  - Suspected ischaemic CRVO with RAPD and VA <6/60  
  - Any new eye pain / red eye (neovascular glaucoma)  
  - Vitreous haemorrhage obscuring view  
  - Raised IOP >25 mmHg  
- **Call medical or stroke team for:**  
  - BP >180/110 mmHg with symptoms (hypertensive emergency)  
  - Suspected GCA: start steroids and contact rheumatology/ophthalmology  
  - New neurological symptoms (stroke or TIA) – consider urgent brain imaging  
- **NEWS2 threshold for
